query: What does ICD-10 stand for and how is it defined?

retrieved_docs.title: Stark Law -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The term "referral" means a request for a specific or service by a physician for Medicare Part B services and/or a care plan that includes designated health services. Also, the term "financial relationship" includes investment interest, ownership, and compensation arrangements.

retrieved_docs.title: Utilization Management -- Definition/Introduction -- Retrospective Review
retrieved_docs.content: Retrospective review is the type of UM that occurs after the care was delivered and after the bill for that care was submitted. The retrospective review seeks to confirm that the care was appropriate and was provided at the most efficient and effective level. Additionally, the retro review also determines if the codes used to describe the care listed on the submitted bill are coded correctly according to such standards as Current Procedural Terminology (CPT) and the International Classification of Diseases-10 (ICD-10). Ideally, the retrospective review should uncover only minimal discrepancies with information that might be available from the pre-auth and concurrent review processes when available. The retrospective review also provides an opportunity to collect data related to the quality of care, compliance with national standards, and additional outcomes data that can be shared with providers and throughout the organization.

retrieved_docs.title: Sarcopenia -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Additionally, sarcopenia is strongly associated with a greater incidence of falls and increased fracture risk. [8] Furthermore, decreased muscle mass or muscle function, both criteria for sarcopenia, are risk factors for loss of independence in patients over the age of 90 years old. [9] Sarcopenia has become an increasingly studied condition and recently received a specific International Classification of Disease (ICD) 10 code to better distinguish it from similar or co-existing diseases that manifest with muscular wasting.

retrieved_docs.title: Hospice Appropriate Diagnoses -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Enrollment into hospice service is not as simple as a patient’s primary physician making a prognosis of fewer than six months to live. On the contrary, many details need to be considered and meticulously assessed, and documented for a patient to transition into hospice successfully. As with each new set of guidelines, revised standards, or updated ICD codes, there are further changes to the interpretation and guidance of hospice diagnoses and regulations. Similarly, there is a consistent rolling out of new Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) expectations regarding billing and coding.

retrieved_docs.title: Medicare and Medicaid Accreditation and Deemed Status -- Definition/Introduction -- Definitions
retrieved_docs.content: Deemed status is a designation granted to healthcare facilities accredited by an approved accrediting organization, such as The Joint Commission, that CMS has recognized as meeting the requirements for participation in Medicare and Medicaid. This means that the healthcare facilities with deemed status do not need to undergo a separate survey by CMS to become eligible for participation in Medicare and Medicaid programs, as the accrediting organization has already assessed the facility for adherence to CMS standards. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Postconcussive Syndrome -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Currently, there are two clinical criteria used to diagnose PCS. These are the International Classification of Diseases, 10th revision (ICD-10), and the DSM-IV, both of which can give varying results, even when applied to the same population. [5] Therefore, a wide range of reported incidence in the literature is reported from 30 to 80 percent of patients with mild to moderate TBI experiencing signs and symptoms of PCS.

retrieved_docs.title: Inclusion Body Myositis -- Introduction -- Classification Criteria
retrieved_docs.content: Clinicopathologically defined IBM: Mandatory criteria + one or both of the clinical criteria plus 1, 2, and 3 of the pathological criteria Clinically defined IBM: Mandatory criteria plus all clinical criteria plus one or more, but not all the pathological criteria Probable IBM: Mandatory criteria plus one clinical criterion plus one or more, but not all the pathological criteria

retrieved_docs.title: Patient Self-Determination Act -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: IN THE HOUSE OF REPRESENTATIVES April 3, 1990 Mr. LEVIN of Michigan (for himself, Mr. SWIFT, Mr. MOODY, Mr. MCDERMOTT, and Mr. FAUNTROY) introduced the following bill; which was referred jointly to the Committees on Ways and Means and Energy and Commerce A BILL To amend titles XVIII and XIX of the Social Security Act to require providers of services and health maintenance organizations under the Medicare and Medicaid programs to assure that individuals receiving services will be given an opportunity to participate in and direct health care decisions affecting themselves. Be it enacted by the Senate and House of Representatives of the United States of America in Congress assembled, SECTION 1. SHORT TITLE. This Act may be cited as the `Patient Self Determination Act of 1990'. SEC. 2. MEDICARE PROVIDER AGREEMENTS ASSURING THE IMPLEMENTATION OF A PATIENT'S RIGHT TO PARTICIPATE IN AND DIRECT HEALTH CARE DECISIONS AFFECTING THE PATIENT.

retrieved_docs.title: Patient Self-Determination Act -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: (b) APPLICATION TO HEALTH MAINTENANCE ORGANIZATIONS- Section 1876(c) of such Act (42 U.S.C. 1395mm(c)) is amended by adding at the end the following new paragraph: `(8) A contract under this section shall provide that the eligible organization shall meet the requirements of section 1866(a)(1)(Q) in the same manner as they apply to hospitals.'. (c) Effective Dates- (1) The amendments made by subsection (a) shall apply with respect to services furnished on or after the first day of the first month beginning more than 180 days after the date of the enactment of this Act. (2) The amendment made by subsection (b) shall apply to contracts under section 1876 of the Social Security Act as of the first day of the first month beginning more than 180 days after the date of the enactment of this Act

retrieved_docs.title: SWOT Analysis -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The concept of strategic fit, a ubiquitous objective sought by all organizations, can be explained as to how well the internally-related factors fit with the externally-related factors. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: CEAP Classification of Venous Disorders -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: During the Fifth Annual Meeting of the American Venous Forum 1993, John Porter suggested a classification for venous disease, just like the TNM (tumor/node/metastasis) classification for cancer. In 1994, the American Venous Forum created a classification system to aid universally uniform diagnosis and comparison of chronic venous disorders. In 1995, the classification was incorporated into the “Reporting Standards in Venous Disease.”  In 2004 the classification underwent revision, which retained the basic CEAP categories but improved the underlying details. The name CEAP classification stands for Clinical (C), Etiological (E), Anatomical (A), and Pathophysiological (P). [1] [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Tricuspid Valve Endocarditis -- Evaluation -- Modified Duke Criteria
retrieved_docs.content: The diagnosis of TVIE is based on the modified Duke Criteria. This criterion is widely used to diagnose TVIE, like all other forms of IE. This criterion allows the stratification of IE into a definite, possible, or rejected category. Based on clinical criteria, definite endocarditis is defined as 2 primary criteria, 1 major with 3 minor criteria, or 5 minor. Possible endocarditis is measured by the presence of 1 major with 2 minor criteria or with 3 minor criteria alone. [11] The criteria classes are as follows:

retrieved_docs.title: Asperger Syndrome -- Introduction -- Historical Perspective
retrieved_docs.content: The 10th Revision of the International Classification of Diseases (ICD-10), introduced in 1993, was the first significant classification system to recognize Asperger syndrome (AS). [7] Then, in 1994, AS was formally introduced as a distinct entity in the DSM-4. It was categorized within PDD alongside autism spectrum disorder, marking an important milestone in recognizing and understanding AS as a separate diagnostic category. While during this period, researchers were focused on developing measures to diagnose AS and differentiate it from high-functioning autism (HFA), the DSM-5 removed the diagnostic category of AS in 2013. [5] The World Health Organization (WHO) also followed a similar approach in ICD-11, which came into effect in 2022. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Denis Classification -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: There have been several proposed classification systems to define thoracolumbar fractures.  In 1983, Francis Denis described a novel classification system to facilitate the communication of these fractures and their treatments among different medical professionals. [1] In his study, he retrospectively analyzed 412 thoracolumbar injuries and developed a three-column theory, replacing the previous two-column theory popularized by Sir Frank Holdsworth. [2] This newly-defined three-column theory would become the foundation for a classification system that would become widely adopted by spine surgeons to formulate treatment algorithms of thoracolumbar injuries.

retrieved_docs.title: Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA) Compliance -- Issues of Concern -- Unique Identifiers Rule (National Provider Identifier)
retrieved_docs.content: The NPI replaces all other identifiers used by healthcare plans, Medicare, Medicaid, and other government programs. However, NPI does not replace a provider's DEA number, state license, or tax identification number. The NPI consists of 10 digits (which may be alphanumeric), with the last digit serving as a checksum. The NPI is devoid of any embedded intelligence and does not carry additional meaning. NPI is unique and national, never reused, and, with the exception of institutions, a provider typically has only 1 NPI. Institutions may obtain multiple NPIs for different "sub-parts," such as a free-standing surgery or wound care center.

retrieved_docs.title: Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act -- Definition/Introduction -- 1976 Medical Device Amendments (PL 94-295)
retrieved_docs.content: These Amendments were introduced in response to a US Senate report that faulty medical devices had caused approximately 10,000 injuries, including 731 deaths. It requires that all medical devices be classified into one of three classes: Class I (General Controls): devices deemed low-risk for human use. Class II (Performance Standards): devices considered moderate-risk for human use. Class III (Premarket Approval): devices deemed high-risk for human use.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 1983
retrieved_docs.content: The concept of “Duration of gestation” is classified within the International Statistical Classification of Diseases and Related Health Problems, 10th Revision, Australian Modification (ICD‑10‑AM), indicating that gestational length is a recognized medical category in this coding system. Within this framework, the specific gestational interval of “29‑30 weeks of gestation completed” is linked to the outcome of a “liveborn” infant, reflecting that infants born after completing 29 to 30 weeks of pregnancy can be alive at birth. Accordingly, the “liveborn” outcome is treated as a distinct subclass or child concept under the broader umbrella of gestational duration, illustrating how gestational age ranges and birth outcomes are organized together in clinical classification and patient‑care documentation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1771
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms are closely related to and may be considered synonymous with the broader category of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions. In this hierarchical framework, diseases and injuries are classified as having the parent category of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, indicating that they are understood through the manifestation of such nonspecific signs. A specific example of a child element within this category is الانهيار في الدورة الدموية (circulatory collapse), which represents a distinct clinical manifestation that can arise from ill‑defined conditions. Together, these relationships highlight that the identification of symptoms and signs, including serious events like circulatory collapse, is essential for recognizing and managing diseases and injuries that present without clear, well‑defined diagnostic criteria.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1714
retrieved_docs.content: The International Statistical Classification of Diseases and Related Health Problems, 10th Revision, Australian Modification (ICD‑10‑AM) organizes medical diagnoses into hierarchical categories, including the Arabic‑named child categories اضطراب تَنَفُّسِيّ (respiratory disorder) and ورم (tumor). Within the broader اضطراب تَنَفُّسِيّ category, the specific adult condition labeled respiratory failure; insufficiency; arrest (adult) is classified as a subordinate entity. This structured taxonomy enables precise coding, systematic reporting, and clearer communication of diagnoses related to respiratory disorders and tumors, thereby supporting accurate documentation and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2503
retrieved_docs.content: The concept of “Duration of gestation” is organized within the International Statistical Classification of Diseases and Related Health Problems, 10th Revision, Australian Modification (ICD‑10‑AM), which also encompasses conditions such as اضطراب تَنَفُّسِيّ (respiratory disorder). Within this classification framework, “liveborn” is regarded as a specific outcome associated with gestational duration; in particular, a gestational period of 29‑30 weeks completed can result in a liveborn infant. This relationship positions liveborn as a concrete instance of the broader category of gestational duration outcomes, illustrating how gestational age, birth status, and disease classification are interconnected in medical documentation and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 4 -- Community 2810
retrieved_docs.content: The International Statistical Classification of Diseases and Related Health Problems, 10th Revision, Australian Modification (ICD‑10‑AM) is a comprehensive coding system used to categorize medical conditions for clinical, epidemiological, and administrative purposes. Within this classification, the category “اضطراب تَنَفُّسِيّ” (respiratory disorder) is listed as a child entry, indicating that various diseases affecting the respiratory system are organized under this heading. Additionally, the category “ورم” (tumor) is also a child entry of the ICD‑10‑AM, encompassing benign and malignant growths of tissues. By structuring these entities under the ICD‑10‑AM, healthcare providers can systematically record, analyze, and manage respiratory disorders and tumors alongside other coded conditions, facilitating accurate diagnosis, treatment planning, and health statistics reporting.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1668

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 4 -- Community 2866
retrieved_docs.content: The International Statistical Classification of Diseases and Related Health Problems, 10th Revision, Australian Modification (ICD‑10‑AM) is a coding system used in Australia to organize diseases and health conditions. Within this system, the category اضطراب تَنَفُّسِيّ is listed as a child entry, indicating that it is a specific diagnostic subgroup under the broader classification. This relationship shows that اضطراب تَنَفُّسِيّ is formally recognized and coded within the ICD‑10‑AM framework, facilitating standardized documentation, reporting, and analysis of respiratory disorders in clinical and administrative settings.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1522
retrieved_docs.content: The medical taxonomy places “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions” as a distinct category that includes the specific manifestation labeled “غشي.” The term “Symptoms” is described as closely related and possibly synonymous with “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions,” indicating substantial overlap in meaning. Moreover, the broader grouping “diseases and injuries” is identified as the parent of “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions,” positioning these vague clinical signs and symptoms as subcomponents within the larger domain of diseases and injuries. This hierarchical and synonymous relationship helps clinicians organize and interpret poorly defined clinical presentations within the overall framework of disease and injury.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1221
retrieved_docs.content: Residual codes; unclassified; all E codes constitute a miscellaneous category within medical coding systems that is used to classify items that do not fit into standard classifications. This residual group is applied to label unspecified elective surgery for purposes other than remedying health states, indicating procedures that are planned but not directed at treating a specific medical condition. In addition, the same residual codes are employed to classify فقدان الشهية (loss of appetite), a clinical symptom that may not have a dedicated code in the primary classification scheme. By serving as a catch‑all for both undefined elective surgical procedures and symptoms such as loss of appetite, these unclassified E codes provide flexibility for recording diverse health‑related events that fall outside conventional coding structures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1826
retrieved_docs.content: The category “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions” is presented as a parent concept that includes the specific child entity “غشي.” In this framework, the general term “Symptoms” is described as closely related to, and potentially synonymous with, the broader phrase “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions,” indicating that the two expressions refer to overlapping clinical manifestations. Consequently, the specific manifestation “غشي” is understood to fall under the umbrella of nonspecific or ill‑defined symptomatology, and clinicians should recognize its inclusion within this broader symptom category when evaluating and documenting patient presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 1020
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia is classified as داء الكريات المنجلية (sickle cell disease). داء الكريات المنجلية is clinically associated with قساح and can lead to respiratory failure; insufficiency; arrest (adult). This form of adult respiratory failure is a subcategory of اضطراب تَنَفُّسِيّ (respiratory disorder), which itself is nested under الاِختِنَاق (asphyxia). Asphyxia is mapped to نقص الأكسجين في الدم (hypoxemia), and نقص الأكسجين في الدم is also a direct manifestation of داء الكريات المنجلية. The International Statistical Classification of Diseases and Related Health Problems, 10th Revision, Australian Modification (ICD‑10‑AM) includes اضطراب تَنَفُّسِيّ and ورم (tumor) as child categories, thereby situating the respiratory complications of sickle cell disease within a broader coding hierarchy. Recognizing these interconnected relationships aids clinicians in identifying that patients with sickle cell disease may develop hypoxemia through asphyxial mechanisms that progress to severe adult respiratory failure, and that these conditions are captured within the ICD‑10‑AM framework for precise diagnosis and management.

retrieved_docs.content: Duration of gestation is a concept that is organized within the International Statistical Classification of Diseases and Related Health Problems, 10th Revision, Australian Modification (ICD‑10‑AM). Within this classification framework, liveborn is treated as a specific category that falls under the broader concept of duration of gestation, indicating that measurements of gestational age are used to define and classify liveborn outcomes. This relationship demonstrates how gestational age information is structured in the ICD‑10‑AM system and applied to the categorization of liveborn infants.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1496
